doom.driver: New outbreak of Ebola kills 80 in eastern DR Congo. Africa's top health agency says around 246 cases have been reported - a case has also been reported in Uganda.
doom.blurb: EBOLA CROSSES THE BORDER, BUNDIBUGYO EDITION
doom.summary: A rare strain of Ebola — Bundibugyo, not the more familiar Zaire — has killed up to 80 people in DRC's Ituri province and is now confirmed in Uganda, meaning it's already jumped an international border. The fact that the US CDC only heard about this yesterday, weeks into a 246-case outbreak, is the kind of institutional lag that makes epidemiologists drink. Meanwhile, Trump and Xi wrapped up a 'very successful' summit with few concrete deals, Putin is immediately flying in to keep Xi's schedule full, and Iran signals it's open to talks while Trump says he's 'losing patience' — so the geopolitical pressure cooker is simmering but not yet boiling over.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: OpenAI shuffles executives and eyes bank account access; Musk v. Altman jury deliberates — corporate drama, not capability explosion.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: DOJ 'Truth and Justice Commission' and possible Raúl Castro indictment signal US institutions being weaponized for political ends.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: Trump-Xi summit yields choreography but no deals; Putin immediately flies to Beijing; Taiwan warnings issued within hours of Xi handshake.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: AI radio stations running unsupervised demonstrate incompetence more than malevolence — threat level: mild cringe.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Bundibugyo Ebola crossing from DRC into Uganda with 246 suspected cases and delayed CDC notification is a textbook early-outbreak warning sign.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing — interior scene, camera positioned deep inside a dim Ugandan border-town clinic waiting room in Busia, looking outward through a wide rectangular doorway framing the red laterite dirt road outside, deep navy shadows dominating the interior, a single wooden bench along the near wall holds folded white isolation gowns and a sealed biohazard transport bag with numbered vials visible through clear plastic, a hand-painted triage chart hangs on the crumbling plaster wall to the left, the doorway rectangle burns with smoldering orange-gold equatorial afternoon haze, outside through the frame a lone figure in a pale blue Tyvek hazmat suit stands motionless on the dusty road facing away, a rusted oil-drum barrier painted in faded colors sits at the road's edge, dense green equatorial scrub presses in on both sides of the road, the sky beyond is a bruised amber-grey, humid heat rendered as shimmer distortion at the horizon, the clinic floor is cracked concrete stained with red mud tracked in from outside, a single bare fluorescent tube flickers overhead casting cold light against the threatening warm glow from the doorway, deep navy and smoldering orange palette throughout, heavy interior shadows, warmth outside feels infectious and threatening.

dawn.driver: First test of CO2 removal with green sand finds no harm to marine life. Adding olivine to the ocean could remove CO2 from the atmosphere, and a pilot project in New York state found no signs of adverse effects on seafloor organisms
dawn.blurb: GREEN SAND CLEANS THE SKY, SPARES THE SEA
dawn.summary: In a world rattled by missile tests, Ebola outbreaks, and geopolitical standoffs, a quiet but significant environmental milestone emerged: a real-world pilot project testing olivine-based ocean CO2 removal found zero harmful effects on marine life. That's not a small thing — it means one of the most promising and scalable carbon removal strategies just passed its first serious ecological safety check. Science, done carefully and honestly, keeps handing us tools to fix what we've broken.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: Ocean-based CO2 removal via green sand olivine showed no adverse effects on seafloor organisms in a real-world pilot, a critical safety milestone for atmospheric cleanup.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: The olivine pilot represents a convergence of geology, marine biology, and climate science working together toward a practical carbon drawdown solution.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: China and the US, despite broader tensions, are reportedly coordinating oil market responses to cushion Middle East supply shocks for the global economy.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: Teens competing in the 'World Series of Birding' remind us that the next generation is paying close attention to the natural world and choosing to protect it.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: NASA's new AI space chip showing strong performance could eventually allow deep-space probes to make autonomous discoveries far beyond human communication delays.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Wide aerial view looking down at a 45-degree angle, vast Atlantic coastal shallows seen from high above at golden hour, warm amber and deep teal light flooding the scene from the low western sun, long shadows stretching across the seafloor visible through translucent shallow water. The ocean bottom itself is the subject — a broad sandy expanse 160 metres offshore from a Long Island beach, where the sediment glitters with unmistakable apple-green and bottle-green mineral flecks scattered throughout pale beige sand, visible even from this height as a luminous greenish band radiating outward from the shoreline. The seafloor teems with tiny second-order life responding to the changed environment: dense pixel clusters of small marine worms, miniature crustaceans, and bottom-dwelling organisms going about their business undisturbed, the ecosystem visibly intact and busy. A lone research vessel sits anchored in the middle distance, impossibly small against the vast glittering shallows, a single figure barely visible at the stern. Waves near the shore carry green-flecked surf foam shoreward in thin luminous lines. The green mineral shimmer catches the golden-hour side-light like scattered sparks across the teal seafloor, radiant and quietly alive.
